skill.name: deployment-automation
skill.description: Guides deployment automation for Docker, GitHub Actions, Kubernetes, Vercel, and blue-green release workflows.
skill.summary: Create deployment pipelines, container workflows, and release checklists for common cloud and server targets.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly true positives for deployment command examples, secret references, network checks, and local file edits, but they appear in instructional templates rather than hidden executable code. No evidence found of prompt injection, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent. The skill should publish with a warning because following the commands can change production infrastructure and touch secrets.

medium_findings.title: Powerful Deployment Commands in User-Facing Templates
medium_findings.description: The skill provides Docker, GitHub Actions, Kubernetes, Vercel, and blue-green deployment commands. These are legitimate for the skill purpose, but unsafe execution could alter live services, push images, or deploy to production.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed shell and workflow commands are plainly present and can mutate infrastructure if followed. The context is deployment documentation, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Secrets and Environment Variables Require Careful Handling
medium_findings.description: The examples reference GitHub tokens, SSH deployment secrets, Kubernetes secret keys, Vercel environment variables, and .env files. They use normal secret mechanisms, but users must avoid exposing real credentials in generated files or logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Secret-related identifiers are directly referenced across deployment examples. The examples generally point to secret stores instead of literal secrets, which lowers the severity.

medium_findings.title: Local File Mutation During Blue-Green Switching
medium_findings.description: The switch script reads current_color.txt, rewrites nginx.conf with sed, and updates the color state file. This is expected release automation, but it can produce incorrect routing or configuration changes if used without validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file read and write operations are explicit in the script. The pattern is legitimate for blue-green deployment, but it is risky when applied to production configuration.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs and Local Health Check Are Benign
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are official documentation links and a localhost health check. No evidence found that the skill sends credentials or project data to an external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible references or localhost checks with no data-bearing request body. This supports a false-positive assessment for network exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Relative Path Links Are Not Active Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The ../ paths appear only as markdown links to related skills. No evidence found of code that reads arbitrary filesystem paths through those references.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path traversal pattern is present in markdown link targets only. There is no active file access primitive at these lines.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detector Appears Unconfirmed
low_findings.description: The flagged weak-cryptography locations do not show a cryptographic operation in context. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1 hashing, or security-sensitive cryptographic use in the reviewed text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain the skill description and a redirect destination, not cryptographic code. This makes the static finding very likely to be a false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell and Deployment Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include docker, kubectl, docker-compose, Vercel CLI, curl, sed, and GitHub Actions run steps. These should be reviewed before execution against real environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and operationally sensitive. They are shown as deployment examples, not as hidden code run by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Store and Secret References
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples reference GitHub Actions secrets, Kubernetes secretKeyRef, Vercel environment variables, and .env handling. These are appropriate patterns but require careful user configuration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Secret references are clear in the examples. They use normal secret-management placeholders and do not include literal credential values.

content.value_statement: Manual releases are slow and easy to misconfigure across environments. This skill helps create repeatable deployment workflows for containers, CI/CD, Kubernetes, and frontend platforms.
actual_capabilities: Drafts Dockerfiles and .dockerignore files for Node.js applications.
Creates GitHub Actions workflows for test, build, image push, and SSH deployment steps.
Outlines Kubernetes deployment, service, autoscaling, and rollout commands.
Provides Vercel and Netlify deployment guidance with environment variable handling.
Builds release checklists for pre-deployment, deployment, and post-deployment validation.
limitations: Examples are templates and must be adapted to the target repository and infrastructure.
It does not verify cloud permissions, cluster state, DNS, or production readiness by itself.
Commands can change live infrastructure if executed without review.
Secret values must be supplied through approved secret stores outside the skill text.

use_cases.title: Create a First CI/CD Pipeline
use_cases.description: Generate a basic workflow that tests, builds, publishes, and deploys an application from the main branch.

use_cases.title: Standardize Container Releases
use_cases.description: Prepare Docker and Kubernetes deployment templates that separate build, rollout, health check, and rollback steps.

use_cases.title: Improve Production Release Safety
use_cases.description: Create deployment checklists and blue-green release steps for safer production changes.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Basic Deployment Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small web application.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a deployment plan for my Node.js web app. Include Docker, GitHub Actions, required secrets, and a simple rollback checklist.

prompt_templates.title: Build a GitHub Actions Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a GitHub Actions workflow for test, lint, Docker image build, container registry push, and production deployment through SSH.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Kubernetes Manifests
prompt_templates.prompt: Create Kubernetes deployment guidance for my app with replicas, resource limits, secret references, service exposure, health probes, and rollout verification.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Zero-Downtime Release Process
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a blue-green deployment process with health checks, traffic switching, rollback timing, monitoring gates, and manual approval points.

output_examples.input: Deploy a Node.js API from GitHub to a production server.
output_examples.output: A staged release plan with container build steps, GitHub Actions jobs, required repository secrets, SSH deployment steps, and rollback checks.

output_examples.input: Move a containerized service to Kubernetes.
output: Deployment manifest guidance with replicas and health probes.
Rollout commands and post-deployment verification tasks.

output_examples.output: A blue-green release checklist covering preview deployment, health validation, traffic switch, monitoring, and rollback window.

best_practices: Review every generated command before running it against shared or production infrastructure.
Store credentials in GitHub Actions secrets, Kubernetes secrets, or platform secret managers.
Use versioned image tags, health checks, monitoring, and documented rollback steps for every release.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real secrets into prompts, generated files, logs, or deployment notes.
Do not deploy from unreviewed generated workflows directly to production.
Do not rely on the latest image tag as the only production release identifier.

faq.question: Can this skill deploy my application automatically?
faq.answer: It provides deployment templates and commands. A user or automation system must review, configure, and execute them.

faq.question: Which platforms does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Docker, GitHub Actions, Kubernetes, Vercel, Netlify-style frontend deployment, and blue-green deployment workflows.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with production secrets?
faq.answer: Do not place production secret values in prompts or generated files. Use platform secret stores and review all references.

faq.question: Does it validate my cloud account or cluster?
faq.answer: No. It gives templates and checks, but it cannot confirm permissions, cluster health, DNS, or account configuration by itself.

faq.question: Can Codex or Claude Code adapt the examples to my repository?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide non-secret repository details, deployment targets, runtime, and environment names for more specific guidance.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked medium risk?
faq.answer: It contains legitimate commands that can change infrastructure and references secret stores. Users should review commands before execution.
